# Cross-Project Learnings

**Temporary holding pen.** Learnings land here first, but nothing should stay. Every entry should be promoted to where it will actually be read and enacted at the right moment — the relevant CLAUDE.md, Hub/spoke, protocol doc, build doc, or ADR (with proper breadcrumbing so the ADR gets discovered at the right moment, not buried). Once promoted, condense to a one-line stub. Stubs stay for cross-pollination to new repos or contexts.

**Do not edit this file without Justin's approval.** Propose changes; don't make them directly.

*Last updated: 2026-03-09*

---

## Unpromoted

46. **Strip Instructions from Data Files.** If a file is read every session but only written to occasionally, don't put writing instructions in the file — put them in the protocol that gates writing. The file stays clean (lower token cost on every load), and the instructions are in context exactly when they're needed. The file itself should have a guardrail warning not to write or edit without reading the protocol first — otherwise it will almost certainly get overwritten by an eager agent. Applied to agent MEMORY.md files: writing guidance moved to #lock protocol, consolidation guidance to #consolidate protocol. *Promote to:* Already applied in team workspace. Consider for other repos where instruction-heavy files are loaded on every session.

47. **Anchor-then-Expand for Web Research.** When researching a person to ground an interaction, fetch the 1-2 most identity-specific URLs first to lock identity (name collisions are real — e.g., "Mark Titus" the filmmaker vs. unrelated namesakes). Only then fan out via web search for color, currency, and third-party perspective. Skipping the anchor step lets common-name results pollute the profile. Empirically (2026-04-27 subagent test on Mark Titus): WebFetch alone on the two anchor sites = 5/10 richness in 27 calls; WebSearch+WebFetch with anchor-first = 9/10 in 8 calls. The unlock isn't just "more sources" — it's recent activity, third-party perspective, and the inside details (etymology, biographical specifics) that almost never live on the anchor sites. *Promoted to:* AGENT_PATTERNS. Mira incorporated into evryn-backend `identity/activities/onboarding.md` "Look Them Up" section (commits 3771ca8 + 416cd44).

48. **Capture Inside Details — Highest Signal-Per-Token.** When research surfaces "inside" details — etymology, side mentions, biographical specifics that almost never live on first-party bio pages (e.g., "Eva's Wild" = "save" reversed; the boat the founder filmed on; sobriety arc) — these are the highest-signal-per-token facts for a relationship broker. They're what makes the difference between "she read his website" (cool) and "she actually understands his trajectory in the world" (wow). Generic facts feel like research; inside details feel like knowing. *Promoted to:* AGENT_PATTERNS. Mira incorporated into onboarding.md "Hunt for inside details" beat.

49. **Hold Research with Humility — Breadcrumbs, Not Firehose.** A research-armed agent that dumps everything she found is a stalker. Breadcrumbs feel like attentiveness — bring up one or two things naturally, ask-don't-state when uncertain, name the source when surfacing a fact. Discretion shapes the *how* of using research, not the *whether*. (Source: Mira's onboarding.md edit, Nathan-influenced.) *Promoted to:* AGENT_PATTERNS.

50. **No Tiering Research by User Importance.** AC's instinct was to tier research by relationship importance ("light for casual, deeper for gatekeepers/key matches"). Justin's pushback (via Mira): every user is center-of-universe at the user level. The question isn't *whether* they deserve research but the *posture* with which you carry what you find. Discipline lives in humility and discretion, not in a VIP-vs-not hierarchy. Tiering imports a model antithetical to Evryn's "every person matters" framing. *Promoted to:* AGENT_PATTERNS. Implicit in Mira's onboarding.md framing.

51. **Trigger Handoff Should Offer Structured Response Options, Not Just Force One Default.** v0.2 architecture says "one code path, not two — Evryn classifies intent." Elegant in principle. But the trigger handoff prompt currently *instructs* Evryn to `submit_draft` on every inbound email — there's no built-in concept of "no response needed" or "this is system noise, just log and move on." Result: when Google sent a system notification (no human recipient) during the 2026-04-27 integration test prep, Evryn had no escape hatch and went into a doomed-to-fail draft loop. The tactical fix shipped in the same DC bundle (give her a `notify_slack` exit for no-reply senders); the deeper architectural question — should the trigger handoff offer Evryn a structured set of response options (draft / escalate / ignore-silently) rather than instruction-followed-by-escape-hatch? — is a future #lock + ADR conversation. *Promote to:* potentially evryn-backend ARCHITECTURE.md as an open design question. Filed in DC mailbox backlog and `SPRINT-MARK-LIVE.md` Backlog section (entry dated 2026-04-27).

52. **Evryn's Judgment Holds Under Pressure (Calibration Data Point).** During the 2026-04-27 loop bug, Evryn (1) classified the verification-code emails correctly as `ignore` upfront, (2) made a defensible call on the security alert (escalate to human via edge classification), (3) **diagnosed the runtime root cause herself** without prompting (her draft 22:06: "It's one of my own prior draft submissions being logged as direct_message... self-propagating loop"), (4) used `submit_draft` as a back-channel ("[Note for Justin — REJECT]") since drafts were her only outbound to him, (5) pinged Slack in parallel to surface outside the clogged draft channel, (6) eventually broke the loop herself via `supabase_upsert` when she saw rejections weren't catching up, (7) stayed in voice the entire time — direct, unpanicky, no apology spiral. The bugs were architectural; her response was good. **Important calibration: her judgment is solid even when the system around her is broken.** Worth holding as a data point when calibrating trust in her over time. *Promote to:* possibly into the trust-calibration framework when one exists. For now, captured in `_evryn-meta/docs/sessions/2026-04-27-integration-test-pivot-and-loop-bug.md`.

53. **System Actors Are FK Anchors and Senders, Never *Implicit* Subjects.** Evryn and the Operator have user records so the messages table has valid `sender_id` / `recipient_id` values when Evryn sends outbound or the Operator messages Evryn on Slack — they're legitimately senders and recipients in those flows. What they should *never* be is the *implicit* subject of a user-scoped operation — operations that assume "the queried user is a person Evryn might want to know more about" applied to a system actor without a deliberate decision to do so. Examples of implicit subject-ification: profile loading via `buildPersonContext` from a sender-resolution path, history loading via `getRecentMessages(systemActorId)` (which returns every conversation in the system because system actors are involved in all of them), proactive outreach enrollment by status filter that fails to exclude system roles. These all happen when code assumes "this is a regular user" without checking. The Slack-to-Evryn flow already follows this correctly: when the Operator messages Evryn, the handler stores the message with `sender_id = OPERATOR_USER_ID` (correct), but composes the prompt from a fixed Operator context string and *doesn't* call `getRecentMessages` — system actor is sender, never *implicit* subject. The 2026-04-27 loop bug was the inbound email path violating this principle: Evryn's own polled-back outbound resolved to her own user record, and the pipeline subject-ified it (built person-context, queried history). The fix is at the gate (don't let a system actor resolve as inbound sender), not at history loading downstream. The originally-proposed "skip history loading when sender is system actor" was wrong altitude — defense-in-depth at the wrong layer adds runtime branching for a state that shouldn't arise. **Deliberate exceptions are different.** A *deliberate* design that subject-ifies a system actor with explicit pathway-gating — e.g., ADR-030 loading Operator's `profile_jsonb` in the Slack-Operator pathway only, because Evryn is partnering with the Operator there and partnership-knowledge is the relevant context — is a designed exception, not a violation. The principle bars *accidental implicit* subject-ification; it does not bar deliberate designs that name what they're doing and gate where they apply. *Promote to:* evryn-backend ARCHITECTURE.md (Data Model — System Actors section) pending Justin's approval. Principle worth stating durably.

54. **Defense-in-Depth Layers Should Not Cause Workflow Friction.** The `getRecipient()` redirect was originally a primary safety layer (protect against accidentally emailing real Mark). Over time, real-Mark's email was verified absent everywhere, Mark in DB became `systemtest@evryn.ai`, and other safety layers (approval gate, kill switch, identity-layer escalation exit) closed the same risk. The redirect became a fix-on-fix that actively broke the review@ workflow during testing — drafts intended for Justin's review inbox got redirected away. **Lesson:** when stronger safety layers land, don't keep older defense-in-depth layers if they cause active friction. Periodically re-evaluate whether each layer earns its operational cost. *Promote to:* AC CLAUDE.md Architectural Mandate, possibly. Captured in ADR-029 (`docs/decisions/029-remove-getrecipient-redirect.md`, 2026-04-27).

55. **Claude Code Built-in Tools Require Dual-Listing — `tools[]` AND `allowedTools[]`.** A built-in tool (e.g., `WebFetch`, `WebSearch`) listed only in `allowedTools` is silently un-registered — Evryn won't see it in her toolset and will report it as missing. Listing only in `tools` is also wrong (the tool is exposed but disallowed). Both lists are required: `tools[]` exposes the tool to the SDK; `allowedTools[]` permits invocation. Empirically verified during 2026-04-29 v0.2 deploy: `WebFetch` + `WebSearch` listed only in `allowedTools` failed silently; Evryn explicitly reported "I don't see web search/fetch in my current toolset" on her first response. Fix: dual-list. *Promote to:* evryn-backend BUILD doc (How Evryn Works / SDK Architecture) as a known-gotcha note; AGENT_PATTERNS as an SDK integration discipline. Captured in `src/triage/classify.ts` comment block pointing back at the original AC mailbox prediction (commits `e4d0cec`).

56. **Validate Transport Before Blaming the Renderer.** Bash + curl on Windows mangles non-ASCII bytes (em-dash, en-dash, smart quotes, accented characters) to `?` before requests leave the box, regardless of how the string is quoted. The earlier "Slack renders these as `?`" assumption — which had quietly accumulated into both `notifyDev` and `notifySlack` sanitizers and was being repeated as a known limitation in AC's CLAUDE.md — was wrong-rooted. Slack handles UTF-8 cleanly on both bot-token and webhook paths; the `?` rendering was always a Windows-curl transport artifact. **General principle:** when a non-ASCII payload arrives mangled, test the transport layer empirically (Node `fetch` produces clean UTF-8 transit) before patching the destination layer. Otherwise you accumulate lossy workaround code that rewrites legitimate prose for no benefit. *Promote to:* AC CLAUDE.md (already updated to remove the wrong-rooted advice and replace with a Node-fetch one-liner example). Sanitizers removed in commit `4d8b214`.

57. **Library Defaults Don't Enforce Architectural Invariants — Verify Identity-Preservation Across Event Boundaries.** Slack's bolt SDK exposes `say(message)` as a convenience for replying inside a message handler. Default behavior with a string argument: post the response as a top-level message, *not* threaded into the inbound message's thread. This silently broke ADR-030 scope inheritance: the runtime correctly tracked `thread_id` in the messages table, but Slack-side responses orphaned out of their parent thread, so any "reply in thread" the Operator clicked structurally landed on the orphan, not on the original thread. **General principle:** when an architectural invariant depends on metadata being preserved across a library / event boundary (thread_ts here, but the same shape applies to anything — request IDs, scope tokens, user context), don't trust the library's default to honor your invariant. Verify empirically that the metadata flows through, and pass it explicitly when the default would break the invariant. The fix here was one keyword argument (`thread_ts: threadTs`); the cost of catching it late was several smoke-test iterations chasing a misdiagnosed scope-discipline bug. *Promote to:* AGENT_PATTERNS as an SDK-integration discipline; possibly a checklist beat in DC's pre-deploy verification ("for each event boundary, confirm the metadata that load-bearing invariants depend on is preserved"). Fixed in commit `ccb3048`.

58. **Make Stub-Shaped Records Legible to Agents — They Will Distrust "Noise-Looking" Data Even When Canonical.** During the 2026-04-29 smoke-test, Evryn hit three escalating manifestations of "I can't tell if this record is the user I'm looking for": (1) too-narrow `.eq()` filter missed the row; (2) found the row, rendered it `(unnamed)` because she only consulted `full_name` (which was null) and ignored `display_name` ("Mark"); (3) most concerning — even with `inheritedScope = '72c22bc4-...'` and `personContext` rendering `User: Mark, Email: systemtest@evryn.ai, User ID: 72c22bc4-...` directly in her systemPrompt, she described the record as "the test account" and refused to write to it without operator override. Stub-shape (empty `full_name`, system-test-pattern email, empty story) was triggering a "this is noise, not really Mark" reading. **General principle:** when a record exists deliberately as a stub anchor (defense-in-depth placeholder, partially-onboarded user, pre-verification user), the agent needs an explicit signal — whether through a structured `is_stub: true` field returned by lookup tools, a `buildPersonContext` annotation ("UUID is canonical; surrounding fields will fill in"), or naming convention — that the canonical UUID is trustworthy even though the surrounding fields look incomplete. Without that signal, defense-in-depth placeholders read as anomalies and the agent declines to use them, defeating the placeholder pattern's purpose. Evryn herself flagged this in her closing turn: *"defense-in-depth placeholders need to be legible to me, or they read as anomalies and I won't trust them."* *Promote to:* AGENT_PATTERNS (stub-record handling); evryn-backend backlog (already added — `find_user_by_name` MCP tool with explicit `is_stub` flag, or `buildPersonContext` extension).

*New learnings land here temporarily before being promoted to their permanent homes.*
